It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Can the hidden hand of the market be an effective and legitimate regulator? The case of animal welfare under a labeling for consumer choice policy approach Christine Parker * Melbourne Law School, University of Melbourne, Parkville, Victoria, Australia Rachel Carey Food Policy Research Group, Faculty of Veterinary and Agricultural Science, University of Melbourne, Parkville, Victoria, Australia Josephine De Costa University of Melbourne, Law, Parkville, Victoria, Australia Gyorgy Scrinis School of Agriculture and Food, Faculty of Veterinary and Agricultural Science, University of Melbourne, Parkville, Victoria, Australia Abstract In Australia, labeling for consumer choice, rather than higher government regulation, has become an important strand of the policy approach to addressing food animal welfare. This paper illustrates the usefulness of “regulatory network analysis” to uncover the potentials and limitations of market-based governance to address contentious yet signiﬁcant issues like animal welfare. We analyzed the content of newspaper articles from major Australian newspapers and ofﬁcial policy documents between 1990 and 2014 to show how the regulatory network inﬂuenced the framing of the regulatory problem, and the capacity and legitimacy of different regulatory actors at three “ﬂashpoints” of decisionmaking about layer hen welfare in egg production. We suggest that the government policy of offering consumers the choice to buy cage free in the market allowed large-scale industry to continue the egg laying business as usual with incremental innovation and adjustment. These incremental improvements only apply to the 20 percent or so of hens producing “free-range” eggs. We conclude with a discussion of when and how labeling for consumer choice might create markets and public discourses that make possible more effective and legitimate regulation of issues such as layer hen welfare. Keywords: animal welfare, consumer governance, food labelling, media content analysis, regulatory network analysis. 1. Introduction Shoppers are entitled to know what they are buying and how it was produced… Shoppers who don’t wish to support unethical egg production systems should vote with their dollars, as this is one instance where supply is deﬁnitely driven by demand (Loughnan 2012, p. 71). It is deeply inured in contemporary neoliberal societies that people can use the market to govern capitalism through consumer choice (Shaw & Black 2010). This is especially true for food (Klintman & Bostrom 2004; Guthman 2007; Roff 2007; Hartlieb & Jones 2009). Pollan (2006), for example, famously encourages consumers to “vote with their fork” and “say no” to food produced using intensive, industrial, chemical-based methods. As a result, the marketing and information claims on retail food packaging have become a focus of conﬂict and contestation over a range of issues from the risks of genetic modiﬁcation to the protection of locally distinctive small-scale production and the advance- ment of ecological sustainability (Ilbery & Maye 2007; Morgan et al. 2006; Albert 2009). Does conﬂict and contestation instigate a race to the bottom in which producers engage in more and more misleading claims to obtain the beneﬁt of a higher animal welfare halo on their food labels? Or can it lead to incremental improvement, large-scale transformation of the market, and public pressure for government regulatory reform? Animal welfare for layer hens is a signiﬁcant issue. By the 1960s, cage farming of eggs had become the predominant method of egg production in the Western world. Often considered the most industrialized and cruelest of animal food production practices, “conventional” or “battery” cage farming has been contested by animal rights activists ever since (Harrison 1964; Brambell 1965; Miele 2011; Veissier & Miele 2014). The European Union [EU],1 New Zealand (National Animal Welfare Advisory Committee [NAWC] 2012), and some American states (see Lovvorn & Perry 2009; Mench et al. 2011)2 have all mandated the slightly more humane “enriched” or “colony”3 cages in preference to conventional cages. A few European countries have gone so far as to ban all cage systems for egg production.4 However, in 2000, Australian government primary industries authorities (who are responsible for regulating agriculture and animal welfare) rejected banning conventional cages (Standing Committee on Agriculture and Resource Manage- ment Working Group [SCARM] 2000). Instead they chose to leave it to industry to label eggs as cage, barn, or free range in order to enable informed choice about hen welfare by consumers in the marketplace (SCARM 2000, pp. 10–11). In essence, the animal welfare policy choice was to be left to the “hidden hand of the market,” to adapt Adam Smith’s famous metaphor (Smith, p. 456). By 2014, “free-range” labeled eggs represented up to 40 percent of retail eggs sold in Australia, up from 14.5 percent in 2005 and 8 percent in 2001 (Australian Egg Corporation Ltd [AECL] 2014).5 Consumers believe that in buying these eggs, they are supporting higher animal welfare, but the meaning and use of this term has become increasingly contentious and has been the topic of ongoing policy debates and initiatives since 2000, as this article will show (Choice May 2012). Even when ofﬁcial government policy leaves contentious issues to the market, this does not leave the issue un-regulated. Rather there are always networks of public and private actors who inﬂuence and regulate market con- duct more or less directly, and more or less effectively or legitimately, regardless of whether government has designed and implemented speciﬁc regulatory policy or not. By the same token, even when government does regulate on a speciﬁc issue, the shape and impact of that regulation will depend upon how it interacts with a range of other public and private regulation that all seeks inﬂuence over the same practices. This paper uses “regulatory network analysis” to uncover the impact of the hidden hand of the market on how the governance of layer hen welfare has continued to evolve through contestation, co-optation, and competition between a range of business, government, and civil society actors (e.g. Shearing 1993; Braithwaite & Drahos 2000; Scott 2001; Schneiberg & Bartley 2008; Abbott & Snidal 2009; Eberlein et al. 2014). The ﬁrst part of the paper explains this “regulatory network analysis” approach and its usefulness in analyzing the capacity and legitimacy of plural public and private actors who interact to co-create regulation of animal welfare in egg production in the broader context of “regulatory capitalism” (Levi-Faur 2012). Regulatory network analysis recognizes that the important regulatory question is not whether some issue, such as whether hens should be kept in conventional cages, is or is not regulated. The question is how is it governed, by whom, and to what effect? These questions are of great signiﬁcance for consumers, non-governmental organizations (NGOs), government authorities, and the various large and small industry players who each depend on markets in different ways to achieve their goals and aspirations. Yet the answers, and their implications for legitimate and effective resolution of contentious policy goals, are quite often opaque. The second part of the paper sets out our methodology for answering the research questions identiﬁed: content analysis of newspaper articles from major Australian newspapers and ofﬁcial policy documents between 1990 and 2014. The third part reports our results and analysis by reference to each of three key government decisions or “ﬂashpoints” for policy contestation between different actors: the decision not to ban cages (2000), the decision not to promulgate mandatory food labeling standards for higher animal welfare labels (2011), and, ﬁnally, the successful intervention of consumer protection authorities who attacked some “free-range” labels as misleading and deceptive (2014). We show that the continuation and wide appeal of animal advocate and consumer pressure has resulted in ongoing contestation of private regulatory standards and the partial co-optation of consumer regulators and powerful supermarkets to the cause of greater regulation of animal welfare labeling. As of 2014, the situation was still in ﬂux. No one regulatory actor has the capacity to dominate the regulation of free range and the space will be subject to ongoing C. Parker et al. The article concludes with a discussion of the potentials and limitations of market-based governance to address contentious yet signiﬁcant issues like animal welfare. We suggest that the government policy of offering consumers the choice to buy cage free did lead to incremental improvement within a “business as usual” approach for the 20 per- cent or so of Australian hens who lay “free-range” labeled eggs. The high public proﬁle of “free-range” egg labeling in the market and in public debate over the meaning of “free-range” created by the labeling for consumer choice gover- nance approach now presents an opportunity (yet to be realized) for further improvement. Such improvement would require a larger scale disruption to industry practice, which could occur via further incremental shift in the accepted baseline for market practice or, more hopefully, further buildup of public and political pressure for a ban on battery cages in Australia to follow the EU, New Zealand, and some American states, as the Australian animal welfare standard for layer hens is currently under review (Animal Health Australia 2016). Public understanding of and contention over the conditions in which layer hens live, prompted by debate over “free-range” labeling, may well contribute to the buildup of this political pressure. 2. Revealing the hidden hand in the market: Regulatory network analysis 2.1. Regulatory network analysis Much of the policy-oriented literature on regulation asks the question: When is regulation effective or not at adequately constraining markets? This implies that the question is about how regulatory interventions inﬂuence otherwise stable markets. Our analysis, by contrast, uses regulatory network analysis, an emerging approach to regulatory studies, to suggest that we should consider markets (such as the market for eggs) as a set of changing relations in which public and private standards are continually being created, adjusted, solidiﬁed, or destroyed. The question is how does a net- work of government, market, and civil society actors interact and change over time to create the plural regulatory gov- ernance standards governing any particular area at any particular time, and what is their impact? Who bears the burdens and beneﬁts? How do the array of regulatory options available tend to open up or close down the possibility for further critique and/or resolution of substantive policy issues? This can help activists and policymakers redirect their regulatory attention. It can also help see more clearly what consumer choice means. Regulatory network analysis is an emerging approach to policy analysis developed by regulatory studies researchers (Braithwaite & Drahos 2000; Cashore et al. 2011; Gulbradsen 2014; Levi-Faur 2012; Eberlein et al. 2014). It draws attention to the way that the regulatory governance of any particular area at any particular time is the result of ongoing interactions (contests, conﬂicts, alliances, modeling, and mimicry) by multiple actors (government, industry, and civil society), at multiple levels (local, national, and global), each seeking to exercise power legitimately and effectively (Scott 2001). Our regulatory network analysis combines the conceptual tools in Abbott and Snidal’s (2009) market, govern- ment, civil society “governance triangle” and Eberlein et al.’s (2014) framework for analyzing transnational business government interactions. As Abbott and Snidal (2009) point out, regulatory standard setting now increasingly occurs via a multiplicity and diversity of schemes with varying participation by a decentralized range of state, business ﬁrm, and NGO actors. The role of government and intergovernmental organizations is often more one of “orchestration” via soft law than man- date via command and control regulation. They suggest an analytic model for conceptualizing regulatory space as a tri- angle in which state, business ﬁrm, and NGO actors interact with each other to varying degrees to set standards under different regulatory standard schemes. Abbott and Snidal (2009) and others (see Meidinger 2006/2007) apply this style of regulatory network analysis to describe transnational business regulation, and to suggest opportunities for business government interaction to overcome failures of international business regulation. As this article shows, their conceptual framework is equally applicable to revealing how government, industry, and civil society co-create regulatory space within a nation state, as in the market for eggs in Australia (noting that fresh eggs cannot be imported into Australia), where there is little formal law or regulation and a proliferation of competing voluntary codes. Abbott and Snidal helpfully analyze the strengths and weaknesses of different schemes along a number of dimensions, pointing out that the capacities and interactions of the various actors will determine the degree of democratic participation and engagement (2009, pp. 554–558), and the degree to which voluntary action by either ﬁrms or public audiences (such as consumers) can be relied upon to achieve policy goals (2009a, pp. 560–563). (2014, pp. 6–7) further specify what questions we must ask in order to understand how govern- ments, NGOs, and businesses interact to co-create regulatory space. They suggest that it is useful to disaggregate reg- ulatory governance into six components: (i) framing the regulatory agenda and setting objectives; (ii) formulating rules or norms; (iii) implementing rules within targets; (iv) gathering information and monitoring behavior; (v) responding to non-compliance via sanctions and other forms of enforcement; and (vi) evaluating policy and providing feedback, including review of rules.For each component they suggest six questions: 1 Who or what is interacting? 2 What drives and shapes the interactions? 3 What are the mechanisms and pathways of interactions? 4 What is the character of interactions? 5 What are the effects of interaction? 6 How do the interactions change over time? Eberlein et al. (2014) argue that it is valuable to specify the mechanisms by which actors interact in performing con- crete regulatory tasks and techniques because it is these interactions that explain the regulatory capacity, performance, and outcomes of each regulator and the whole network. The signiﬁcance of these interactions go beyond the regulatory outputs to the very shape of the market – what products are available, and how they are produced and sold. It is important to understand the dynamics of regulatory governance networks because markets and regulation (by which we mean business-government-NGO regulatory interactions) continually constitute one another. Levi-Faur (2012; see also King & Pearce 2010) draws on Polanyi’s (1944) theory of how economies are embedded in sociopolitics to suggest that the proliferation of plural and networked regulation is not holding markets back, but is the essence of capitalist innovation. Contem- porary capitalism takes the form of “regulatory capitalism” in which new markets – and hence the range of con- sumer choice – are created and old ones destroyed through new networks of public and private governance. Whenever there is conﬂict and contention over values or policy goals in markets, there is conﬂict and contention over regulation, and how it is legitimate or not to exercise regulatory power. Whenever anyone seeks to challenge existing market practices they must eventually use the various components of regulation identiﬁed by Eberlein et al. (2014) to challenge existing power relations, cultures, and technologies in order to be successful. Conversely, those who beneﬁt from the existing system will seek to use regulation to respond to the challenge while preserving their position (King & Pearce 2010). 2.2. Food labeling and regulatory network analysis Food labeling is a particularly fruitful area to study this interaction. The food label – broadly deﬁned to include the mar- keting and branding on the packaging and retail display of food – has become a space for a range of public and private regulatory claims and contests to be played out (Parker 2014; Scrinis & Parker 2016). Investigation of the value chain that connects food production and consumption inevitably uncovers the importance of networks of public and private regulation and governance in sustaining and challenging the values and practices underlying the chain (Nestle 2003; Guthman 2004; Lang & Heasman 2004; Morgan et al. 2006). Regulation – formal legal-contractual arrangements, logo accreditation schemes, legislative frameworks, and informal conventions – is integral to stabilizing those chains and deﬁning which values, interests, and assumptions about how food production should be organized prevail in different links in the chains. There are a host of private attempts to standardize and regulate the claims made on food labels, in- cluding various voluntary accreditation logos and standards; supermarket, hospitality, and food processors’ supply standards; and attempts by various advocacy organizations to inﬂuence both government and private regulatory at- tempts (Fulponi 2006; Havinga 2006; Meidinger 2008; Fuchs et al. 2011). This makes it increasingly important to look behind the label (see also Bartley et al. 2015) at the network of regulation and governance that inﬂuences what is put on the label. Which stakeholders are most vocal? What interests and regulatory policy options do they represent? And how do they interact to inﬂuence and change the choices put to consumers in the market over time? By revealing the politics and governance of the label, we can also reveal the politics and governance at work in the market. While much work has been done uncovering the politics behind nutritional and health front of pack food labeling (Scrinis & Parker 2016; Scrinis 2016), less has focused on higher animal welfare and other quality claims (see Buller & Roe 2013). Research questions For the purposes of this paper we focus particularly on Eberlein et al.’s (2014) ﬁrst aspect of regulatory governance: framing the regulatory agenda and setting objectives. This is because at stake in this case study is the very framing of the issue of the caging of hens in egg production as a regulatory issue, and if so, what sort of issue. Regulatory studies literature often assumes that there is agreement on a substantive policy goal (e.g. less use of cages for hens) and then examines, evaluates, and compares different government, private, and hybrid governance mechanisms for implementing and achieving the goal. The case study in this paper, however, is a situation where the policy goal is at best vague (animal welfare and consumer choice) and in practice hotly contested (ban cages or not). This leaves the issue of animal welfare open to contestation as to what regulatory standards under what type of regulatory agenda would apply – industry, NGO, or retailer standards for animal welfare and on farm practice? Consumer protection prohibitions against misleading and deceptive labeling? Or the potential to re-open the possibility of legislated animal welfare protections? We focus particularly on three of Eberlein et al.’s (2014) speciﬁc questions: Who or what is interacting; what is the character of the interaction; and what is the effect of the interaction? We also examine how the interactions change over time. We use the term “ﬂashpoint” to point to the way in which contestation and conﬂict between regulatory actors and regulatory policy proposals at each of these points in time lead to a particular outcome. Yet the network of interaction means that these outcomes continue to unfold over time.6 3. Methodology 3.1. Data sources This paper tracks the interactions between the changing actors, discourses, and regulatory mechanisms mentioned in reported public policy debate about the ethical governance of animal welfare in egg production from 1990 to 2014. The data for this regulatory network analysis are all publicly available ofﬁcial policy documents and reports, and newspaper articles from major Australian newspapers concerning the ethics and governance of animal welfare in egg production and consumption from 1990 to 2014. The comprehensive collection of ofﬁcial policy documents concerning animal welfare in egg production and related labeling issues were used to create a timeline of policy events and to record the debates and discourses that were used to justify various policy developments over time. The timeline was then used as the basis for deﬁning search terms to col- lect all newspaper articles in major Australian newspapers digitally archived in Factiva concerning governance of animal welfare in egg production and consumption.7 This resulted in a total of 712 articles. It is an inherent limitation of relying on newspaper articles as a data source that they do not necessarily capture all the stakeholders and discourses that may have been inﬂuential behind the scenes in private lobbying or in other fora that are not reported.8 However, the comprehensive tracking of regulatory policy documents means that we were able to ensure that all policy discussions with stakeholder involvement that led to a formal outcome were considered, with an emphasis on those who were most vocal in public discourse. Newspaper articles also do not capture increasingly im- portant social media debates and discourses; however, as our aim was a comparison over time, it was important to use a stable data source from across the time period. 3.2. Data analysis The articles were coded using NVivo for any mention of different stakeholders and regulatory policy options. The codes were grouped and statistics produced using NVivo.9 The basic coding categories used for analysis of the newspaper articles were developed on the basis of the comprehensive collection of ofﬁcial policy documents and preliminary qualitative analysis of media articles. These codes were tested with a preliminary sample then reﬁned and applied to the whole data set. The article content lends itself to quantitative analysis to identify which stakeholders and regulatory policy options are used when – and, thus, compare the dominance of different stakeholders and regulatory policy options at different times. Further qualitative analysis identiﬁes and assists an understanding of the discourses used by various stakeholders to justify their policy positions, the terms of debate, and the interaction between the various actors. This approach has much in common with studies of how media frame news and social issues (see e.g. Nisbet et al. 2003; Kitzinger 2007). Here, however, the focus is on how regulatory actors, policy options, and discourses interact and appeal to the public as reﬂected in newspaper articles (following Almond 2009; Van Erp 2013). In 2000, federal and state agriculture ministries acting collectively as the Standing Committee of Agriculture and Resource Ministries (SCARM) determined not to ban conventional cages. SCARM stated that its objective was the: …quality assured health and welfare of fowls in a commercially viable, competitive and environmentally friendly egg industry producing a regular, reliable and affordable supply of eggs allowing for informed choice by consumers and according to contemporary standards for food safety and occupational health (pp.3–4). It recommended legislating a small increase in cage sizes and setting up an industry-based quality assurance and labeling standard that included clearer labeling of cage, barn, and free-range eggs. Alongside their campaigns to ban conventional cages, animal welfare groups had long advocated that in the absence of a ban on battery cages, consumer-citizens should buy free-range labeled eggs. Indeed, as early as January 1980, The Sydney Morning Herald reported that the NSW Minister for Agriculture had agreed to the sale of eggs labeled “free range” to distinguish them from battery cage eggs on the request of Animal Liberation (Anonymous 1980; see also Roberts 1985). By 2002, the non-mandatory government Model Code of Practice for the Welfare of Animals: Domestic Poultry brieﬂy mentioned cage, barn, and free-range options for egg production. SCARM’s proposal accepted the industry position while palliating animal welfare concerns by proposing to “let all consumers vote with their shopping trolleys at the supermar- ket checkout” (Anonymous 2000). The resulting labeling for consumer choice approach was a government attempt to coordinate between the otherwise highly conﬂicting views of industry and civil society. 4.1.2. Impact of interaction on regulatory policy and capacity. The immediate impact of the SCARM decision was to largely remove the possibility of banning conventional cages and requiring enriched cages from the public policy agenda. As Figure 4 shows, in the period leading up to 2000 (and reaching a peak around 1999/2000), relevant Figure 3 Flashpoint 3: 2014, the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission takes enforcement action against misleading and deceptive free range labelling) (n = 74). Labeling for consumer choice C. Parker et al. © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 9 Page 10: newspaper articles were predominantly concerned with whether or not to ban battery cage production. This issue dropped right off the agenda in 2001 with labeling and misleading and deceptive conduct issues increasingly becoming more important (as we shall see). SCARM and mainstream industry succeeded in shifting the interaction from a high conﬂict debate over whether to regulate production by banning battery cages to a general agreement that regulation should facilitate consumer ability to choose cage free if consumers wished. The second impact of the SCARM decision was to open up the regulatory space to a variety of voluntary certiﬁca- tion and labeling schemes aimed at substantiating “free range” claims on retail eggs. The earliest higher animal welfare voluntary accreditation scheme had been the RSPCA’s “approved farming” standard for layer hens in 1996. This focused on cage-free indoor “barn” production.12 The Free Range Farmers Association, an association for small-scale, alternative producers, introduced a “free range” accreditation and logo scheme in 1998. In 2003, in response to the 2000 SCARM Report, the Australian Egg Corporation (AECL), the industry association representing mainstream egg pro- ducers, introduced its own ofﬁcial quality assurance scheme, “Egg Corp Assured,” which included minimal free range production and labeling requirements suitable for large-scale conventional egg producers. Two further alternative higher animal welfare certiﬁcation and labeling schemes covering layer hens were introduced in 2006: Humane Choice (offered by an animal welfare organization with small-scale producer involvement) and Australian Certiﬁed Organic. This proliferation of competing free-range accreditation schemes also created the conditions for contestation and confusion in the ensuing years. SCARM recommended that the AECL’s quality assurance scheme receive legislative backing, that methods of production for labeling be legislatively deﬁned, and that labeling standards be set under federal consumer protection legislation or state labeling legislation (Standing Committee of Agriculture and Resource Ministries [SCARM] 2000, p. 27). However, no deﬁnitions of the different production methods or labeling standards were introduced in legisla- tion. As we shall see, there may have been broad acquiescence to the labeling for consumer choice approach to animal welfare, but this was not the end of conﬂict. The meaning of “free range” labeling claims were increasingly contested in the following years with Green members of parliament (MPs) and activists calling for legislation to mandate labeling and deﬁne the term “free range” (see Figure 5). The third impact of the decision not to ban battery cages related to the hens themselves. The policy frame shifted from the possibility of incremental regulatory improvement in animal welfare for all layer hens (e.g. by requiring enriched cages with the potential for further improvements in years to come) to making available cage-free eggs to satisfy one segment of the market. This postponed the possibility for improvement in animal welfare for the vast majority of hens, namely those that produced eggs for consumers still willing to buy retail cage eggs and those eggs pro- duced for hospitality, catering, and processed foods. The former now amount to about 60 percent of retail eggs, while the latter amount to just over half of the eggs produced in Australia and are mostly cage as they are subject to less Figure 4 Number of mentions of different regulatory options for regulating layer hen welfare in major Australian newspapers, 1990–2014. C. Parker et al. Labeling for consumer choice 10 © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd Page 11: scrutiny by consumers (Outlaw 2012).13 (There has been little discussion in the Australian newspapers of the possibility of contesting the use of cage eggs in fast food outlets like McDonalds [see the hospitality section in Figures 1, 2 and 3].) Thus, SCARM’s labeling for consumer choice approach to higher animal welfare standards postponed or shut down the possibility of removing cages for about 80 percent of egg producing hens in Australia. 4.2. 2011: Mandatory food labeling standards for animal welfare rejected 4.2.1. Regulatory actors and character of interaction. By 2007, newspaper debate about animal welfare labeling of eggs had become preoccupied with discussion of the meaning of “free range.” Figure 5 compares the number of mentions of cage, barn, and free-range production systems in newspaper articles between 1990 and 2014, showing an increase in discussion of the term “free range.” The government agriculture ministries’ adoption of the labeling for consumer choice approach had left the regulatory ﬁeld ripe for competition between different free-range labeling and certiﬁcation schemes. Moreover, the transmutation of the possibility of higher animal welfare regulation by government into a consumer choice had also opened up the possibility that a broader range of civil society and consumer groups would enter this debate. By 2007, the constellation of stakeholders mentioned in the newspaper articles expanded to include consumer advocacy NGOs, consumer regulators, and supermarkets, in addition to those concerned with animal welfare and primary production. Figure 2 shows the ﬁgures for 2011, at which time the Australian government’s food labeling review rejected the possibility of introducing mandatory labeling standards for higher animal welfare claims (as discussed further). In 2008, consumer advocacy group, Choice, gave the Australian Egg Corporation a “shonky award” for their deﬁnition of “free range,” involving an outdoor stocking density of 20,000 hens per hectare, many times more than the 1500 hens per hectare in Australia’s non-mandatory Model Code of practice for the Welfare of Animals: Domestic Poultry and the 2500 per hectare deﬁnition of free range legislated in the United Kingdom (Burke 2008; Department for Environment, Food, & Rural Affairs 2002). In 2009, NSW Green Senator, John Kaye, issued a press release using the egg industry’s own statistics to question whether there were really enough free-range hens in Australia to produce the number of free-range labeled eggs being sold: he suggested that there was either widespread substitution of cage or barn for free-range eggs or that what was being called “free-range” production was closer to barn with minimal access to outdoors (Burke 2009). Three years later, the AECL admitted that it had indeed certiﬁed “free-range” operations with stocking densities of 50,000 hens per hectare and minimal outdoor access (AECL 2012a). The key issue of contention became whether “free range” should be deﬁned in a way that facilitated large-scale supply at reasonable cost or whether it should be deﬁned in a way that sees it as a smaller scale, premium – and higher welfare – alternative to most of the eggs (cage, barn, or even “free-range” labeled) available in major supermarkets. Parker et al. © 2017 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 11 Page 12: By 2009, a number of newspaper articles reported that free-range producer and accreditation organizations (such as Humane Choice and the Free Range Farmers Association), smaller pasture-based producers, and certain specialist re- tailers (particularly organic stores and farmers markets) advocated a small-scale, pasture-based version of free range in which birds spend most of their time out on the range with indoor housing an option when required. Features of this method of production typically included low ﬂock sizes; low stocking densities (both outdoors and indoors); movable housing; signiﬁcant groundcover, shelter, and enrichment on the range; and the use of Maremma Sheepdogs to protect the hens from foxes (e.g. Wilson 2010). This contrasted sharply with the intensive barn-based systems used by the larger scale “free-range” egg producers who supplied the major supermarkets. These systems house ﬂocks of tens of thou- sands of hens in large barns with nesting facilities and feed and water inside the barn. Doors or small popholes are opened to afford some of the birds access to a range area, but they may not be open for very long each day, the range is often bare and unattractive to the birds, and it may be very difﬁcult for most birds to make their way through the barn to the pophole or door (e.g. Smith 2011). Moreover, the birds are de-beaked to avoid pecking and cannibalism in their crowded condition. Some large-scale producers argued that either cage or barn-based production was better than free range for animal welfare (because of the protection from predators), health and safety (protection from infection from wild birds and other dangers outside and the ability to control conditions inside), and cost efﬁciency to enable con- sumer choice. Their perspective is discussed further in the third ﬂashpoint. By 2011, Green MPs, consumer and animal activists, and some smaller scale producers were advocating for man- datory labeling laws to be introduced throughout Australia (e.g. Smith 2011). The small jurisdiction of the ACT had introduced a mandatory requirement to clearly label and separate cage, barn, and free-range eggs in all stores in 2001.14 The EU had also mandated labeling of the production method (organic, free-range, barn, or cage) for retail eggs since 2008, with each method of production legislatively deﬁned.15 A comprehensive federal government review of food labeling regulation and policy in 2011 appeared to afford an opportunity to introduce similar requirements in Australia. The Food Labelling Law and Policy Review Panel (2011) (“the Blewett Review”), however, determined that animal welfare was a low risk, “consumer values” issue that did not warrant mandatory labeling requirements nor legislated deﬁnitions of the claims used on labels (see also Bruce 2012; Food Standards Australia and New Zealand 2013). Rather, animal welfare labeling should be a matter of self-regulation and enforcement of the general consumer laws prohibiting misleading and deceptive conduct. Thus, neither agriculture departments, responsible for regulating animal welfare, nor the food standards authority, responsible for regulating food labeling standards, were willing to coordinate between competing conceptions of free range and animal welfare.
